query: Testbed for complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Angular TestBed considered harmful!
results.content:
And hide it when `visible === false`. And I should not test that. That's Angular's job and I trust it and its thousands of tests. There are thousands of people looking on the framework's code, chances are bugs will get caught and fixed. I need to test MY code. That's what is usually broken because there are only one pair of eyes on it and zero unit tests until I actually create them. [...] What I’m getting at is test only our code and let Angular do its job. That is to take the values and bind them to HTML. Which means we do not need to actually compile our components and have TestBed render them. Only to instantiate the underlying class and test the instance’s methods and properties.

Taking the approach of not rendering HTML coerces us into not having logic in the templates because we would not be able to test it! And not having logic in templates is a very nice thing!

TestBed is not required [...] context and allows us to override providers, services, and whole modules. It compiles, instantiates, and renders to HTML our components attaching them to the fixture instance.
   It wants to compile and instantiate our components and then execute tests against the DOM. This forces us, the test authors and maintainers to query DOM and work with the HTML which is the output of Angular. I find that slow, quirky, and most importantly — not our job. I trust Angular that when given a variable that is `visible === true` to actually visualize my component:

results.title: Unit Testing in Angular - To TestBed or NOT to TestBed - DEV Community
results.content:
## Less Flaky Tests

Without TestBed, we are no longer testing the DOM itself which means that changes to the DOM will no longer break your tests. I mean how many times have you created a component somewhere in your Angular application all of a sudden tests start failing? This is because TestBed is creating the DOM `beforeEach` test. When a component and its dependencies are added its parent component will now fail.

`beforeEach`

Let's take a look at this more in-depth by creating a parent component called `MyParentComponent` with `ng g c my-parent`

`MyParentComponent`
`ng g c my-parent`

Now let's take a look at the `my-parent.component.spec.ts` file:

`my-parent.component.spec.ts`

### WITH TestBed [...] TestBed was just to slow and adding all modules for dependencies was really tedious.

It’s interesting though, that there is a speed up to be expected with Angular 9.

jordanpowell88 profile image

Their is a noticeable speed improvement with IVY (version 9+). It is nice to have the option to use TestBed when you want to but the main point of the article was that I don’t believe it is necessary in almost all use cases and in the end isn’t then truly a unit test

martinspire profile image [...] flamesoff profile image

Testing with TestBed is NOT unit, it's Integrational. It's a different level of testing.  
And yes, after many years of development I came to conclusion that testing with the TestBed is a pure waste of time.   
In the same time unit tests are complete opposite, unit tests are crucial, helpful and easy to maintain.

mrgrigri profile image

At my work we've adopted TypeMoq with out the use of TestBed and it's been amazing.

razorblade446 profile image

I think is not accurate to say that TestBed is a waste of time, moreover when an Angular Component is strongly tied to HTML.

If you have logic in your component that does not need to interact with HTML, I would suggest that logic to go in a Service.

results.title: What is complaint management? Key steps + tips
results.content:
Whatever the reason, try to show some empathy without deflecting the blame or defensive posturing. A little human connection can defuse tension. This sets the stage for productive problem-solving and offers an opportunity to showcase your commitment to customer satisfaction.

AI tools can analyze agents’ messages and adjust the tone to make them more friendly or formal as needed.

### 3. Reiterate the problem

Ever played the game “telephone” as a kid? One rotation around the circle, and “the cat sat on the mat” becomes “the fat rat ate the hat.” Miscommunication happens, but when it comes to complaint management, it can worsen an already frustrating situation for customers and potentially damage their relationship with your company. [...] Even if the issue seems glaringly obvious, restate it back to the customer in an appropriate tone of voice to ensure you fully understand it. Ask clarifying questions if needed—you don’t want to waste time proposing solutions for the wrong problem. This step eliminates any miscommunication and gets everyone aligned.

AI tools integrated into your agents’ workspace can streamline this process by summarizing the customer’s issue, topic, and sentiment accurately.

### 4. Investigate what went wrong and why

Once you’ve clarified the problem, it’s time to play detective. Look into the customer’s history, review any notes or recordings, and determine exactly where the breakdown occurred. This isn’t about pointing fingers but rather understanding the root cause. [...] AI-powered quality assurance tools can help highlight problem spots and provide insights into agent gaps, so you can improve service quality and reduce churn.

By thoroughly investigating, you’re not just solving the current issue—you’re preventing future ones. This step is an opportunity to turn a negative into a positive, transforming customer frustration into valuable insights that can improve your entire operation.

### 5. Follow up with the customer and make it right

After your investigation, loop back with the customer by explaining what went wrong in easy-to-understand terms. Be transparent about the steps you’ve taken and any changes implemented. If you’ve fallen short, own it and make it right with a gesture that fits the situation.

results.title: Unable to unit test Angular component with effect without TestBed · Issue #54438 · angular/angular · GitHub
results.content:
No

### Description

# Background information

I prefer to unit test without TestBed. Arguing whether or not TestBed is good to use in pure unit tests (not integration tests) is outside the scope of this bug report.

When unit testing components without using TestBed, I instantiate my component instance directly, i.e. `new MyComponent()`. In older versions of Angular, this just worked fine as if your component required any dependencies, those dependencies were made clear in the constructor parameters.

`new MyComponent()` [...] `runInInjectionContext`
`new MyComponent()`
`NullInjectorError: NullInjectorError: No provider for InjectionToken !`

After looking into it further (requiring a reading of the source code), I found this:

angular/packages/core/src/render3/reactivity/effect.ts

Line 254
in
dd8490f

|  |  |
 --- |
|  | injector.get(APP\_EFFECT\_SCHEDULER), effectFn, |

OK, so I just need to provide a mock value in my unit test for `APP_EFFECT_SCHEDULER`, right?

`APP_EFFECT_SCHEDULER`

Wrong.

Going to definition on that symbol brought me here:

Basically this means it is IMPOSSIBLE to mock this, which makes it IMPOSSIBLE to use effects in unit tests without TestBed.

### Please provide a link to a minimal reproduction of the bug

### Please provide the exception or error you saw [...] ### Please provide the exception or error you saw

`NullInjectorError: NullInjectorError: No provider for InjectionToken !
error properties: Object({ ngTempTokenPath: [ 'InjectionToken ' ] })
at NullInjector.get (
at R3Injector.get (
at effect (
at new MyComponent (
at eval (
at runInInjectionContext (
at UserContext.eval (
at <Jasmine>`

### Please provide the environment you discovered this bug in (run `ng version`)

results.title: CFPB Announces Major Overhaul of Consumer Complaint System: A Shift Toward Integrity, Standardization, and Statutory Compliance | Consumer Finance Monitor
results.content: NCLC also disputes the Bureau’s suggestion that rising complaint volumes primarily reflect abuse by credit repair organizations, social media influencers, or AI-driven tools. Instead, NCLC maintains that the large number of complaints demonstrates the persistence of serious problems in the credit reporting system and reflects widespread inaccuracies and consumer frustration with existing dispute processes. The organization has argued that, rather than imposing additional barriers to filing complaints, the CFPB should focus on ensuring that consumer reporting agencies adequately investigate and resolve disputes.
